title: N-Queens Puzzle with Diagonal Conflicts and Overcounting

statement.markdown: Given a board of size n, place n queens on the board such that no two queens attack each other. However, we have a bug in our backtracking algorithm that results in overcounting. The task is to fix the bug to produce the correct count of ways to place n queens on an nxn board.
statement.inputFormat: The input will be an integer n specifying the size of the board.
statement.outputFormat: The output should be the total number of ways to place n queens on the board.
statement.notes: Time complexity should be O(n!) due to backtracking and space complexity should be O(n) for the board and O(n) for the recursion stack.

examples.explanation:
There are two ways to place 4 queens on a 4x4 board: 
1. queen at (0,0), queen at (1,2), queen at (2,3), queen at (3,0) 
2. queen at (0,1), queen at (1,0), queen at (2,3), queen at (3,2)

hints.text: First hint text: Review the code's recursion and backtracking logic to identify the bug causing overcounting.

editorial.title: N-Queens Puzzle with Diagonal Conflicts and Overcounting
editorial.intuition: The correct approach involves using a backtracking algorithm to place queens on the board while ensuring that no two queens attack each other. We need to correctly count the number of ways to place queens on the board.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force approach would involve recursively trying all possible placements of queens on the board and checking for conflicts.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves using backtracking to place queens on the board while ensuring that no two queens attack each other. We need to correctly count the number of ways to place queens on the board.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the solution can be proven by showing that the backtracking algorithm correctly identifies all possible placements of queens on the board and counts them correctly.
editorial.dryRun: Step-by-step example run of the correct solution
commonMistakes: Mistake 1: Incorrectly counting the number of possible placements
Mistake 2: Failing to consider all possible conflicts between queens

functionSignature.cpp: bool placeQueen(vector<vector<bool>>& board, int row, int col); int totalNQueens(int n);
functionSignature.java: boolean placeQueen(boolean[][] board, int row, int col); int totalNQueens(int n);
functionSignature.python: def placeQueen(self, board, row, col): def totalNQueens(self, n):
